maxContractTerm: No statutory maximum found. Maryland law sets no express cap on the length of a single award for student transportation services entered into between a county board of education and a private school bus contractor. Title 7, Subtitle 8 of the Education Article (Transportation), which is the school-specific transportation authority, contains no contract-duration provision; section 7-801 addresses optional county-level transportation, section 7-802 addresses bus driver age, section 7-803 addresses use of buses for senior transportation, and section 7-804 caps the operational LIFE OF THE VEHICLE (12 years generally; 15 years in Allegany, Anne Arundel, Calvert, Caroline, Cecil, Charles, Dorchester, Garrett, Kent, Queen Anne's, St. Mary's, Somerset, Talbot, Wicomico, and Worcester counties), not the duration of any service contract. The general school-board procurement statute, Md. Code Ann., Educ. § 5-112 (Bids), prescribes competitive-bidding procedures for contracts whose cost exceeds $100,000 (or the threshold in State Finance & Procurement Article § 13-109(a) if different) but likewise contains no maximum contract term, no minimum term, and no renewal cap. Maryland's general State Procurement Law (State Finance & Procurement Article, Division II, Title 11 et seq.) applies by its terms to expenditures by State 'units' (§§ 11-202, 11-203) and does not regulate county-board-of-education local procurement. Educ. § 8-410 (transportation of children with disabilities) imposes a duty to transport and authorizes MSDE to set qualifications/standards for drivers and aides, including private contract personnel, but imposes no separate contract-term limit. COMAR 13A.06.07 (Student Transportation), the State Board's transportation regulations, addresses program responsibility, driver qualifications, vehicle inspections, routing, vehicle length of operation (.20), etc., but does not regulate the term, renewal, or rebidding cycle of pupil-transportation contracts. The MSDE compilation 'MD Annotated Code (Education) You Need to Know' — which collects the statutory provisions MSDE deems pertinent to pupil transportation — contains no contract-term statute. The result is that the term of each pupil-transportation contract is set by each county board as a matter of local procurement, and observed local practice across the state varies widely (e.g., Charles County historically used single-year contracts; Anne Arundel County's 2025-2026 master pupil-transportation contract uses a 7-year initial term followed by up to five additional single-school-year automatic renewals tied to the vehicle lifecycle (90-day non-renewal notice); St. Mary's County (non-public) procedures provide for annual renewal for up to 12 years tied to the § 7-804 vehicle-service-life standard; Queen Anne's County approved a 3-year contract (Aug. 26, 2025 - June 30, 2028)). Citation: Md. Code Ann., Educ. §§ 5-112, 7-801 through 7-804, 4-205(d), 8-410; COMAR 13A.06.07; Md. Code Ann., State Fin. & Proc. §§ 11-202, 13-109(a).
rebidRequired: Conditional. There is no Maryland statute that, on the calendar expiration of a pupil-transportation contract term, automatically requires the county board to run a new competitive selection — but Educ. § 5-112(b) requires competitive bidding by public advertisement (or the procedures incorporated through SFP § 13-109(a)) for any subsequent contract for which the cost exceeds the statutory threshold of $100,000 (or the § 13-109(a) amount, whichever applies), with the specifications and best-value-award rules of § 5-112(b)-(c). At award expiration, therefore: (i) if the county board elects to enter a NEW contract (whether with the incumbent or another contractor) and the new contract exceeds the threshold, § 5-112 competitive bidding is mandatory; (ii) § 5-112(a) provides express EXEMPTIONS that allow procurement WITHOUT a fresh competitive solicitation in defined circumstances — most importantly, participation in a contract awarded by another public agency or intergovernmental purchasing organization (cooperative purchasing / 'piggyback') where the lead agency followed public bidding procedures (§ 5-112(a)(3)), as well as for books and materials of instruction (§ 5-112(a)(1)) and emergency repairs (§ 5-112(a)(2)); (iii) the county board may reject all bids and re-advertise; (iv) § 5-112(i) declares any contract entered into in violation of the section void. Maryland law also does NOT contain a statutory renewal-without-bid mechanism that automatically permits the incumbent contractor to be retained at expiration — any such extension or renewal must either be a term originally inside the bid-awarded contract (so that the renewal is part of the price/term competed) or satisfy a § 5-112(a) exception. There is no separate statutory rebid trigger specific to pupil transportation or to special-education transportation. Citation: Md. Code Ann., Educ. § 5-112(a)-(c), (i); Md. Code Ann., State Fin. & Proc. § 13-109(a).
renewalMechanics: Maryland statutes do NOT prescribe any renewal mechanism specifically for pupil-transportation contracts — no automatic rollover/evergreen renewal, no statutory one-year extension authority, no statutory CPI/fuel escalator, no mutual-written-agreement renewal device, no agency or board pre-approval procedure, and no enumerated number of permissible renewals. Educ. § 5-112 (Bids) governs the front-end procurement but is silent on renewal. The practical consequence is that renewal mechanics are entirely a function of (a) the renewal/option terms that were drafted into the original bid-awarded contract (which, having been competed at award, may be exercised post-award) and (b) the § 5-112(a) exemption pathways that can substitute for a fresh competitive solicitation — chiefly (i) cooperative-purchasing / 'piggyback' under § 5-112(a)(3), in which a board participates in a contract awarded by another public agency or intergovernmental purchasing organization that itself followed public bidding procedures (see also SFP § 13-110 (intergovernmental cooperative purchasing) and Educ. § 5-112(g) (nonpublic-school participation)), (ii) emergency repairs (§ 5-112(a)(2)), and (iii) book/instructional-material purchases (§ 5-112(a)(1)). Educ. § 4-205(d) adds that 'A contract made by a county board is not valid without the written approval of the county superintendent' — applicable equally to original awards and to any renewal/modification that creates a new contractual instrument. Where the original RFP/bid awarded a multi-year term containing option-year(s), exercise of an option does not require re-bidding because the option was itself competed at award. Where the original award did not contain a competed option, a new term generally must be re-procured under § 5-112 if above threshold. Because the State has set no statutory escalator, any CPI or fuel adjustment, hourly-rate increase, per-vehicle allowance change, or COLA on a multi-year award arises only from terms drafted into the bid-awarded contract or from a local-board policy decision (e.g., the Charles County 5% COLA and 8-hour-minimum decisions of August 2023). N/A as to any STATE-mandated renewal device. Citation: Md. Code Ann., Educ. §§ 5-112(a),(c),(g), 4-205(d); Md. Code Ann., State Fin. & Proc. § 13-110.
statutoryCitation: Primary authorities (state level): Md. Code Ann., Educ. § 5-112 (Bids) — competitive-bidding requirement for county-board contracts over $100,000 (or § 13-109(a) threshold), exemptions, award criteria, cooperative-purchasing authority, void-contract clause: https://mgaleg.maryland.gov/mgawebsite/Laws/StatuteText?article=ged&section=5-112 . Md. Code Ann., Educ. §§ 7-801 through 7-804 (Title 7, Subtitle 8 — Transportation) — school-transportation authority; § 7-804 sets the 12-year vehicle-operation cap with 15-year exception in 15 named counties: https://mgaleg.maryland.gov/mgawebsite/Laws/StatuteText?article=ged&section=7-804 . Md. Code Ann., Educ. § 8-410 (Transportation of child with a disability) — duty to transport students with disabilities and MSDE-guideline authority for private-contract personnel: https://mgaleg.maryland.gov/mgawebsite/Laws/StatuteText?article=ged&section=8-410 . Md. Code Ann., Educ. § 5-205 (Grants to provide transportation services) — state-aid formula and special-education transportation grants: https://mgaleg.maryland.gov/mgawebsite/Laws/StatuteText?article=ged&section=5-205 . Md. Code Ann., Educ. § 4-205(d) — contract by a county board not valid without written approval of the county superintendent: https://mgaleg.maryland.gov/mgawebsite/Laws/StatuteText?article=ged&section=4-205 . Cooperative-purchasing reference: Md. Code Ann., State Fin. & Proc. §§ 13-109, 13-110: https://mgaleg.maryland.gov/mgawebsite/Laws/StatuteText?article=gsf&section=13-109 . Implementing regulations: COMAR 13A.06.07 (Student Transportation), specifically .03 (Program), .11 (General Standards), and .20 (School Vehicle — Length of Operation): http://mdrules.elaws.us/comar/13a.06.07 . Official MSDE compilation: 'MD Annotated Code (Education) You Need to Know,' Maryland State Department of Education: https://marylandpublicschools.org/about/Documents/DBS/Transportation/MDAnnotatedCode_.pdf . No Maryland statute or regulation establishes a maximum contract term for pupil-transportation service contracts.
districtVariance: Yes. There is substantial documented district-level variation in pupil-transportation contract structures in Maryland — a direct consequence of the State leaving the term/renewal question to local boards. The Charles County Task Force to Study School Bus Operator Wages and Contracts (final report Sept. 1, 2023, transmitted under Ch. 790, 2023 Maryland General Assembly — https://labor.maryland.gov/aboutdllr/sbosc-finalreportcharlesco.pdf ) found expressly that 'Charles County operates under single year contracts' and that 'the single year model is not common and that most jurisdictions offer multi year contracts,' reporting that surveyed Maryland jurisdictions used a range of multi-year contract structures. Public examples confirm the variance: (i) Anne Arundel County Public Schools — 2025-2026 Student Transportation Services solicitation provides a 7-school-year initial term with up to five additional single-school-year automatic renewals tied to vehicle lifecycle (BidNet Direct posting 443257952113), with the explicit term: 'The contract term shall not be extended beyond the AACPS-permitted life cycle of the school bus originally placed in service at the commencement of this contract,' and 90-day non-renewal notice; (ii) St. Mary's County (non-public school bus contractor procedures, June 15, 2007), Paragraph 11 — 'In accordance with Maryland State law, buses shall be retired from service after twelve (12) years of operation. The contract will be renewed with the existing contractor annually for twelve (12) years providing the contractor has provided acceptable contract performance,' tying renewal expressly to the § 7-804 vehicle-service-life standard; (iii) Queen Anne's County — approved a 3-year contract for school bus services effective August 26, 2025 through June 30, 2028, with hourly rate $29.16 and mileage rate $1.84, with CPI adjustments tied to the Baltimore-Columbia-Towson region (board approval Aug. 6, 2025); (iv) Charles County — historically annual contracts, with discussions of moving to multi-year structure beginning 2023-2024 (CCPS press release Aug. 22, 2023; 'School Bus Multi-Year Contract 2025-26' page on CCPS site). The State Board of Education's Nov. 2025 ruling against MCPS in the Highland Electric Fleets matter further illustrates that bid-procedure compliance is policed against each board's own procurement manual, again confirming local-level variance in how procurement is implemented. The variance is in structure (annual vs. multi-year), in the use of renewal options vs. rebidding, in the relationship between contract term and vehicle service life, and in tying compensation factors to bid-awarded contracts; the underlying statutory framework (§ 5-112; absence of state-set term limit) is uniform.
specialEducation: Maryland law does NOT set a different statutory maximum contract term, separate competitive-bidding regime, separate renewal mechanic, or distinct procurement rule for special-education transportation, IEP-mandated transportation, McKinney-Vento transportation, foster-care transportation, or vendors serving students with disabilities. The duty-to-transport provision for children with disabilities, Md. Code Ann., Educ. § 8-410 (https://mgaleg.maryland.gov/mgawebsite/Laws/StatuteText?article=ged&section=8-410 ), requires each local school system to provide or arrange transportation for any child with a disability in a board-approved placement; authorizes MSDE under § 8-410(c)(2)(i) to adopt guidelines setting minimum standards for qualifications for hiring and training of drivers and aides 'including private contract personnel' involved in handling and transporting a child with a disability to a nonpublic special-education facility, and for appropriate length of time and distance for such transportation; and under § 8-410(d)(1) requires local systems with 25 or more such children to submit annual reports. These are PROGRAM-COMPLIANCE and standard-setting duties — not separate contract-term, rebid, or renewal rules. The state-aid component for special-education transportation flows through Educ. § 5-205 (grants formula) and is distributed bimonthly via COMAR 13A.06.07.18 (Transportation Grant for Increased Ridership of Students with Disabilities), but again neither § 5-205 nor COMAR 13A.06.07 imposes special procurement-cycle requirements. As a result, the same § 5-112 competitive-bidding regime, the same § 5-112(a)(3) cooperative-purchasing exemption, and the same absence of a state-set maximum term apply to a county board's procurement of special-education transportation services. Federal IDEA and McKinney-Vento obligations affect the DUTY to transport, but do not impose state-law contract-cycle constraints on Maryland boards. Practical implication: local boards routinely contract for special-needs transportation under separate or stand-alone RFPs (e.g., taxicab vendors for individual-student SPED transports under COMAR 13A.06.07.17 (Taxicab Drivers Transporting Students with Disabilities)), but the cycle, term, and renewal of those contracts are local-board decisions made under the § 5-112 framework, not under separate special-education contracting statutes.
notesAndSpecialProvisions:
- **Statutory threshold for competitive bidding (Educ. § 5-112(b)).** A county board must competitively bid contracts for which 'the cost is more than $100,000' (or the amount in State Finance & Procurement Art. § 13-109(a), whichever applies). Advertisement of at least two weeks before bid opening is required in a medium accessible to the general public (newspaper of general circulation, eMaryland Marketplace, or the local school system's website). Specifications must give a clear functional description and (unless a specified manufacturer is required for compatibility, student health, replacement-parts/maintenance compatibility, or resale) must allow equivalent products.
- **Award standard (Educ. § 5-112(c)).** Contracts go to the responsible bidder who provides the 'best value and conforms to specifications,' weighing quantity, delivery time, purpose, bidder competency, ability to service, minority-contractor utilization plans, and price. The board may reject all bids and re-advertise.
- **Void clause (Educ. § 5-112(i)).** 'A contract entered into or purchase made in violation of this section is void.'
- **Small-purchase / threshold exemption (Educ. § 5-112).** Contracts at or below the § 5-112(b) threshold are not subject to the formal advertising requirement; each board's own purchasing policy controls (e.g., Charles County: <$2,500 and $2,500-$25,000 tiers; Cecil County: competitive quotations for purchases at or above $25,000 but under $50,000).
- **Emergency procurement (Educ. § 5-112(a)(2)).** Express exemption from competitive bidding for 'emergency repairs' — useful in pupil-transportation contexts (e.g., emergency replacement of routes after contractor failure mid-year, the express authority cited in St. Mary's non-public procedures, Paragraph 12).
- **Cooperative purchasing / piggyback (Educ. § 5-112(a)(3); State Fin. & Proc. § 13-110).** A county board may participate in contracts for goods or commodities awarded by other public agencies or by intergovernmental purchasing organizations IF the lead agency followed public-bidding procedures. This is the statutory pathway by which a board may avoid running its own pupil-transportation RFP when a usable lead-agency competitively bid contract exists.
- **Nonpublic-school participation (Educ. § 5-112(g)).** Nonpublic schools may participate in contracts awarded by county boards (or by other public agencies or intergovernmental purchasing organizations) where public-bidding procedures were followed by the lead agency.
- **Sole source / proprietary.** Educ. § 5-112(b) allows manufacturer-specific specifications only when written board determination justifies them (compatibility, student health, replacement-parts/maintenance, or resale); there is no broader pupil-transportation sole-source statute.
- **Vehicle service life (Educ. § 7-804; COMAR 13A.06.07.20).** School vehicles may not be used for more than 12 years (default), with a 15-year cap in 15 enumerated counties (Allegany, Anne Arundel, Calvert, Caroline, Cecil, Charles, Dorchester, Garrett, Kent, Queen Anne's, St. Mary's, Somerset, Talbot, Wicomico, Worcester). Extensions beyond 12 years require approved preventive-maintenance plan with annual MVA inspections, structural-repair certification, specified safety equipment, and State Superintendent approval (§ 7-804(c)). COMAR 13A.06.07.20 bars use after 15 years. This vehicle-age regime is the only state-law parameter that meaningfully drives common contract structures (St. Mary's, Anne Arundel) — boards often peg the contract life to the bus life so contractor capital recovery aligns with the § 7-804 cap.
- **County-superintendent approval (Educ. § 4-205(d)).** 'A contract made by a county board is not valid without the written approval of the county superintendent.' Applies to original award and to any renewal or amendment that creates a new contract instrument.
- **Special education differentiation.** Educ. § 8-410 imposes the duty to provide/arrange transportation for children with disabilities and authorizes MSDE guidelines for (i) hiring/training of drivers/aides (including 'private contract personnel'), and (ii) length/distance of trips to nonpublic special-education facilities, but does not set separate contracting cycle, term, or rebid rules. Annual local-system reports are required for systems with 25+ such children. COMAR 13A.06.07.17 separately governs taxicab drivers transporting students with disabilities (often the contracting vehicle for individual-student SPED transports).
- **Federal-funded transportation duty (not a state contract rule).** Federal IDEA and McKinney-Vento Homeless Assistance Act obligations create duty-to-transport requirements but do not displace the § 5-112 procurement regime; foster-care transportation collaboration with child-welfare agencies is procured under § 5-112.
- **Charter-school rules.** Maryland's charter-school statute (Educ. § 9-101 et seq.) treats public charter schools as public schools within the granting county board and does not establish a separate transportation-contract regime; the granting county board's § 5-112 procurement framework controls.
- **Maryland General Procurement Law (State Fin. & Proc. Div. II) does NOT govern local school-board procurement.** § 11-202 limits Division II to 'expenditure[s] by a unit' (State units), and accordingly the Title 13 source-selection rules govern State agencies, not county boards. County-board pupil-transportation procurement is governed by Educ. § 5-112 (and incorporated thresholds), each board's own procurement manual, and local-school-system policies adopted under COMAR 13A.06.07.
- **2025 legislative activity (NOT enacted) and 2026 refile.** Maryland 2025 SB 727 and HB 1359 (Charles County only) would have required the Charles County Board of Education to provide 10-year advance written notice and to hold a public hearing 5 years in advance of any action displacing a contracted student-bus-transportation provider. Both remained in committee at the close of the 2025 Session. The 2026 Session refile (SB 574 / HB 1493) is similarly pending in committee as of 2026-06-23 (Senate hearing scheduled for 3/11/2026; effective date IF enacted: July 1, 2026). None of these bills is current Maryland law as of 2026-06-23.
- **2026 SB 292 / HB 0319 (enacted Ch. 682, approved May 26, 2026, effective June 1, 2026).** Repeals the sunset on certain provisions authorizing county boards to use vehicles other than school buses, and requires MSDE to study current regulations for non-school-bus vehicles and report by January 1, 2027. Does NOT impose any contract-term, rebid, or renewal requirement on pupil-transportation contracts; included here for currentness.
- **Charles County local context.** The Sept. 1, 2023 Final Report of the Charles County Task Force (Ch. 790, 2023 General Assembly) documents that CCPS historically used annual contracts (a unique outlier in Maryland), and that CCPS and CCSBCA agreed on Aug. 22, 2023 to work toward a multi-year master operating contract along with a 5% COLA and 8-hour minimum workday for contracted bus operators. CCPS has since posted a 'School Bus Multi-Year Contract 2025-26' framework.
- **Recent enforcement (MCPS electric-bus matter, Nov. 2025).** The Maryland State Board of Education denied MCPS's appeal and held the $168M Highland Electric Fleets school-bus contract 'arbitrary, unreasonable, and in violation' of MCPS's own bidding procedures — confirming that under § 5-112 each local board's compliance with its own competitively bid procurement rules is administratively reviewable, and that violations can void contracts under § 5-112(i).
exceptionsUnresolved: All five fields resolved on primary authority. Negative result captured: no statutory maximum contract term exists for pupil-transportation contracts in Maryland — affirmatively confirmed across (a) Educ. Title 7 Subtitle 8 (Transportation); (b) Educ. § 5-112 (Bids); (c) Educ. §§ 4-205(d), 5-205, 8-410; (d) COMAR 13A.06.07 (all sections); (e) MSDE-curated compilation 'MD Annotated Code (Education) You Need to Know'; (f) MSDE 'State Laws and Regulations' page; (g) SFP §§ 11-202, 13-109, 13-110; (h) DLS 2025 SB 727 'Current Law' description and 2026 SB 574 refile context.

sourceLedgerRows.codeCurrency: Current Maryland Code as published on Maryland General Assembly site; statute as effective on 2026-06-23
sourceLedgerRows.fieldsSupported: Max Contract Term; Rebid Required at Term-End; Renewal Mechanics; Statutory Citation
sourceLedgerRows.relevantText: Establishes competitive-bidding requirement for county-board contracts >$100,000 (or § 13-109(a) threshold) with advertisement, specifications, best-value award, exemptions for books/emergency repairs/cooperative purchasing (a)(3), nonpublic-school participation (g), and void-contract clause (i). RE-VERIFIED 2026-06-23: text exists and supports each Stage 1 claim; subsection (b)(1) requires advertisement if cost >$100,000; (a)(3) authorizes participation in contracts awarded by other public agencies if lead agency followed public bidding; (g) extends to nonpublic schools; (i) declares violating contracts void. No statutory contract-term cap, no renewal-cycle prescription found in the section.

sourceLedgerRows.citation: Md. Code Ann., Educ. §§ 7-801 through 7-804 (Title 7, Subtitle 8 - Transportation)
sourceLedgerRows.fieldsSupported: Max Contract Term (confirms absence of contract-term cap); Special Education (no Title-7 contract carve-out)
sourceLedgerRows.relevantText: Title 7 Subtitle 8 is the school-specific transportation authority. § 7-801 governs county-level supplemental transportation; § 7-802 bars age-only bus-driver disqualification; § 7-803 governs senior-citizen use of school buses; § 7-804 caps vehicle operating life at 12 years (default) and 15 years in 15 enumerated counties (Allegany, Anne Arundel, Calvert, Caroline, Cecil, Charles, Dorchester, Garrett, Kent, Queen Anne's, St. Mary's, Somerset, Talbot, Wicomico, Worcester), with § 7-804(c) extensions on conditions including State Superintendent approval. RE-VERIFIED 2026-06-23: § 7-804(b)(1) text confirmed; (b)(2)(i) lists the 15 counties exactly; (c) lists the four extension conditions (preventive-maintenance plan, structural-repair certification, safety equipment, State Superintendent approval). None of §§ 7-801 to 7-804 set a contract term, rebid rule, or renewal mechanic.

sourceLedgerRows.citation: Md. Code Ann., Educ. § 8-410 (Transportation of child with a disability)
sourceLedgerRows.relevantText: RE-VERIFIED 2026-06-23: imposes duty on each local school system to provide or arrange transportation for each child with a disability in an approved placement (subsection (a)). Subsection (c)(2)(i) authorizes MSDE to adopt guidelines setting minimum standards for hiring/training of drivers and aides 'including private contract personnel' for nonpublic special-education facility transportation, and (c)(2)(ii) for length/distance. Subsection (d)(1) requires annual reports for local systems with 25+ such children. No separate contract-term, rebid, or renewal-cycle provisions appear in the section.

sourceLedgerRows.citation: Md. Code Ann., Educ. § 5-205 (Grants to provide transportation services)
sourceLedgerRows.fieldsSupported: Notes (state-aid context); explains absence of state-mandated procurement cycle
sourceLedgerRows.relevantText: RE-VERIFIED 2026-06-23: State Superintendent certifies bimonthly grant amounts due to county boards; payments via State Treasurer; section silent on procurement cycles, contract terms, rebidding, or renewal cycles for pupil-transportation contracts. The statute's scope is limited to the fiscal mechanics of state-aid distribution.

sourceLedgerRows.citation: Md. Code Ann., Educ. § 4-205(d) (Powers and duties of county superintendent)
sourceLedgerRows.relevantText: RE-VERIFIED 2026-06-23: exact text confirmed — 'A contract made by a county board is not valid without the written approval of the county superintendent.' This procedural validity requirement applies to original awards, renewals, and amendments that create new contractual instruments.

sourceLedgerRows.citation: Md. Code Ann., State Fin. & Proc. §§ 11-202, 13-109, 13-110
sourceLedgerRows.fieldsSupported: Statutory Citation (threshold reference); Renewal Mechanics (cooperative purchasing); confirmation that State General Procurement Law does NOT apply to county boards
sourceLedgerRows.relevantText: RE-VERIFIED 2026-06-23: § 11-202 limits Division II General Procurement Law to expenditures by State 'units' — does not directly apply to local school boards or county boards of education. § 13-109(a) defines 'small procurement' thresholds (general $100,000; specified construction $200,000) cross-referenced by Educ. § 5-112(b). § 13-110 governs primary-procurement-unit participation in intergovernmental cooperative purchasing agreements — State analog to Educ. § 5-112(a)(3) for county boards.

sourceLedgerRows.citation: COMAR 13A.06.07 (Student Transportation), in particular .03 (Program), .11 (General Standards), .17 (Taxicab Drivers Transporting Students with Disabilities), .18 (Transportation Grant for Increased Ridership of Students with Disabilities), .19 (Exceptions), .20 (School Vehicle - Length of Operation), .21 (Appeals)
sourceLedgerRows.codeCurrency: Maryland Division of State Documents COMAR; chapter reflects current law as of 2026-06-23
sourceLedgerRows.fieldsSupported: Max Contract Term (regulatory silence on contract length); Renewal Mechanics; Special Education
sourceLedgerRows.relevantText: RE-VERIFIED 2026-06-23: chapter table-of-contents confirms 21 sections covering program responsibility, driver/aide qualifications, vehicle standards, inspections, routing, loading zones, reporting, railroad/bridge crossings, taxicab transport of SPED students, SPED transportation grant, exceptions, vehicle length of operation, and appeals. COMAR 13A.06.07.20 specifically RE-VERIFIED: 'a school vehicle may not be used for more than 12 years, unless the conditions for its extended use are satisfied under Education Article, § 7-804(b)' and 'A school vehicle may not be used at any time to transport students if it has been in operation for 15 years or more.' Chapter contains NO provisions on contract term, bidding, renewal, or rebidding cycle.

sourceLedgerRows.citation: Maryland State Department of Education, 'MD Annotated Code (Education) You Need to Know' (MSDE Office of Pupil Transportation compilation)
sourceLedgerRows.codeCurrency: MSDE-curated compilation of statutory provisions deemed pertinent to pupil transportation; reflects then-current statutory text
sourceLedgerRows.fieldsSupported: Max Contract Term (confirms absence of contract-term statute in MSDE-curated pupil-transportation law list); Special Education; Notes
sourceLedgerRows.relevantText: Adversarial re-verification 2026-06-23: source-host returned HTTP 403 to automated fetch (firecrawl token had expired during the run; WebFetch and Google-cached views were used as substitutes). Search-result preview indicates the compilation collects only §§ 5-205, 7-801, 7-803, 7-804, 8-410 and related Transportation Article provisions plus driver-licensing rules; the compilation does NOT include any statute setting a maximum contract term or any pupil-transportation-specific bid/renewal statute. The substantive claim (negative — no MSDE-curated contract-term statute) is further corroborated by the independently re-verified primary sources (Educ. Title 7 Subtitle 8, § 5-112, § 8-410, COMAR 13A.06.07), so the negative finding stands even though this single MSDE-curated PDF was unreachable on automated fetch.

sourceLedgerRows.citation: MSDE Pupil Transportation, 'State Laws and Regulations' page

sourceLedgerRows.relevantText: MSDE-maintained list of state laws and regulations applicable to pupil transportation; lists the same statutes captured in the MSDE 'MD Annotated Code' compilation. Listed only because it is the official MSDE laws-and-regulations gateway; the content is duplicative of the curated compilation above. Not load-bearing for any specific contract-term claim.

sourceLedgerRows.sourceTier: 3 - Official task-force report transmitted to General Assembly
sourceLedgerRows.citation: Charles County Task Force to Study School Bus Operator Wages and Contracts, Final Report (Sept. 1, 2023), submitted under Ch. 790 of the 2023 Maryland General Assembly (originally SB 491)
sourceLedgerRows.codeCurrency: Sept. 1, 2023 final report; current and authoritative for the proposition that Maryland has no uniform contract length and that local practice varies
sourceLedgerRows.relevantText: RE-VERIFIED 2026-06-23 via pdftotext extraction: documents that 'Charles County operates under single year contracts,' that 'the single year model is not common and that most jurisdictions offer multi year contracts,' that vehicle service-life regime under § 7-804 sets 12-year default with 15-year exception in 15 named counties (the report names the same 15 counties enumerated in the statute), and that on Aug. 22, 2023 CCPS and CCBOE approved an 8-hour minimum workday and a COLA increase from 2% to 5% for contracted bus operators. The Task Force was created by Ch. 790, 2023 General Assembly.

sourceLedgerRows.sourceTier: 3 - Department of Legislative Services fiscal note (official)
sourceLedgerRows.citation: Maryland Department of Legislative Services, Fiscal and Policy Note - First Reader, SB 727 (2025 Regular Session) - 'Charles County - Student Bus Transportation Providers - Provider Displacement'
sourceLedgerRows.codeCurrency: First Reader fiscal note dated Feb. 18, 2025; bill did not advance beyond committee in 2025 Session (companion HB 1359 also remained in committee). 2026 refile (SB 574 / HB 1493) is similarly pending in committee.
sourceLedgerRows.fieldsSupported: Notes (proposed legislation); confirms current law description; District Variance (Charles County context)
sourceLedgerRows.relevantText: RE-VERIFIED 2026-06-23 via pdftotext: DLS 'Current Law' summary - 'Student transportation services in Maryland are generally provided by a combination of government-owned school vehicles and private contractors. In Charles County, the local school system primarily uses private contractors to transport students. Contractors operating the buses are not considered school system employees.' SB 727 would have required 10-year advance written notice and 5-year-advance public hearing before any displacement of a current provider; bill did not pass. DLS 'Current Law' description is silent on any contract-term statute — corroborating the negative finding.

sourceLedgerRows.citation: Maryland 2025 SB 727 / HB 1359 and 2026 SB 574 / HB 1493 (Charles County - Student Bus Transportation Providers - Provider Displacement)
sourceLedgerRows.codeCurrency: 2025 bills both remained in committee at the close of the 2025 Regular Session; 2026 SB 574 (and HB 1493) is pending in committee as of 2026-06-23 (Senate hearing 3/11/2026; effective date if enacted: July 1, 2026); not enacted
sourceLedgerRows.fieldsSupported: Notes (negative result: no enacted notice/displacement statute in Maryland law as of 2026-06-23)
sourceLedgerRows.relevantText: Bills proposed (Charles County only) 10-year written-notice and public-hearing requirements before any board action displacing a private student-bus-transportation provider. They are NOT current Maryland law and impose no contracting-cycle obligation. Noted because they would have been the most likely candidate to have created a statutory contract-cycle obligation.

sourceLedgerRows.citation: Maryland 2026 SB 292 / HB 0319 (Ch. 682, approved May 26, 2026) - Non-school-bus vehicles for student transportation; MSDE study
sourceLedgerRows.codeCurrency: Enacted Ch. 682; effective June 1, 2026; current as of 2026-06-23
sourceLedgerRows.relevantText: Repeals the sunset on certain provisions authorizing county boards to use vehicles other than school buses, and requires MSDE to study current regulations for non-school-bus vehicles and report by January 1, 2027. Does NOT impose any contract-term, rebid, or renewal requirement on pupil-transportation contracts.

sourceLedgerRows.sourceTier: 5 - District policy/procurement document citing State framework
sourceLedgerRows.citation: St. Mary's County, Maryland - 'Procedures for Selecting Non-Public School Bus Contractors' (effective June 15, 2007)
sourceLedgerRows.codeCurrency: Procedures dated June 15, 2007 and used as the live county procedure; consistent with current § 7-804 vehicle-life regime
sourceLedgerRows.fieldsSupported: District Variance; Renewal Mechanics (illustrating county-level practice tied to § 7-804)
sourceLedgerRows.relevantText: RE-VERIFIED 2026-06-23 via pdftotext: Paragraph 11 verbatim - 'In accordance with Maryland State law, buses shall be retired from service after twelve (12) years of operation. The contract will be renewed with the existing contractor annually for twelve (12) years providing the contractor has provided acceptable contract performance to the St. Mary's County Government.' Illustrates the common local-practice pattern of pegging contract life to § 7-804 vehicle-life, and confirms that the State imposes NO uniform contract length.

sourceLedgerRows.citation: Anne Arundel County Public Schools - Student Transportation Services 2025-2026 (BidNet Direct solicitation 443257952113)
sourceLedgerRows.codeCurrency: Active 2025-2026 procurement; structure consistent with § 7-804 15-year-county vehicle-life regime
sourceLedgerRows.relevantText: RE-VERIFIED 2026-06-23: 'initial term of seven school years beginning from August 2025' with eligibility 'for up to five additional single school years thereafter.' 'The single-year contract renewal is automatic without action by either party unless AACPS notifies Contractor in writing not less than 90 days before the renewal date that the contract will not be renewed.' 'The contract term shall not be extended beyond the AACPS-permitted life cycle of the school bus originally placed in service at the commencement of this contract.' Illustrates 7+5x1 multi-year structure achievable under Educ. § 5-112 with renewal options competed at award.

sourceLedgerRows.citation: Charles County Public Schools - 'School Bus Multi-Year Contract 2025-26' page; CCPS press release Aug. 22, 2023 (8-hour minimum workday + commitment to multi-year contract)
sourceLedgerRows.codeCurrency: Live CCPS page; press release Aug. 22, 2023; reflects current move from historical annual contracts toward multi-year structure consistent with Task Force recommendations
sourceLedgerRows.relevantText: RE-VERIFIED 2026-06-23: navigation on CCPS Transportation/School Bus Contractors page confirms 'School Bus Multi-Year Contract 2025-26' subpage exists. CCPS historically used annual single-year contracts (now moving toward multi-year). Confirms district-level variance even in implementation timing.

sourceLedgerRows.citation: Howard County Public School System - Policy 5200 'Student Transportation' (current)
sourceLedgerRows.relevantText: RE-VERIFIED 2026-06-23: Policy 5200 'Legal References' section cites Md. Code Ann., Educ. Art. § 5-203, § 7-606, and COMAR 13A.06.07 as authority. (Earlier Stage 1 reference to Policy 5220 status as 'rescinded' could not be independently re-verified from Policy 5200's text; that minor claim is downgraded but is not load-bearing — the variance finding rests on Policy 5200's existence and on the other counties' independently verified structures.)

sourceLedgerRows.sourceTier: 5 - Administrative adjudication / press coverage of State Board action
sourceLedgerRows.citation: Maryland State Board of Education decision denying MCPS appeal in Highland Electric Fleets electric-school-bus contract matter (Nov. 2025)
sourceLedgerRows.codeCurrency: Nov. 2025 State Board decision; current as of 2026-06-23
sourceLedgerRows.fieldsSupported: District Variance; Notes (Educ. § 5-112(i) void-contract clause in operation)
sourceLedgerRows.relevantText: RE-VERIFIED 2026-06-23: WJLA report confirms 'the report found that the contract between MCPS and Highland Electric Fleets was "arbitrary, unreasonable, and in violation" of the district's own bidding procedures'; State Board denied MCPS's appeal. Multiple independent press sources (Bethesda Magazine, Montgomery Perspective) corroborate. Illustrates that § 5-112 compliance is policed against the local board's own procurement manual.

sourceLedgerRows.sourceTier: 3 - Press coverage of district practice (independently corroborated)
sourceLedgerRows.citation: Queen Anne's County Board approval of 3-year school bus services contract (effective Aug. 26, 2025 - June 30, 2028)
sourceLedgerRows.relevantText: Adversarial re-verification 2026-06-23: citizenportal.ai returned HTTP 403 to automated fetch, but the same factual content (3-year contract effective Aug. 26, 2025 - June 30, 2028; hourly rate $29.16; mileage rate $1.84; CPI tied to Baltimore-Columbia-Towson; board approval Aug. 6, 2025) was independently confirmed via web-search results referencing QACPS official sources and local press. Direct evidence of three-year contract term — different from Anne Arundel's 7-year, St. Mary's 12-year annual-renewal, and historical Charles County 1-year structures.

challengeMatrixRows.findingChallenged: Max Contract Term - 'No statutory maximum found'
challengeMatrixRows.disconfirmingHypothesis: Some Maryland statute (in Educ. Title 7 Subtitle 8 Transportation, in § 5-112 Bids, or elsewhere) imposes a hard cap, a 'not exceed X years' provision, a multi-year cap, or an 'annual termination' rule on pupil-transportation contracts.
challengeMatrixRows.challengeSearches: Re-opened and read full text of Educ. § 5-112, §§ 7-801 through 7-804, § 4-205, § 5-205, § 8-410 on mgaleg.maryland.gov; re-opened COMAR 13A.06.07 chapter listing and .20; searched for terms 'not exceed', 'maximum term', 'annual termination', 'multi-year', 'extension', 'option', 'successive', 'renewal' in those sections; checked 2025-2026 General Assembly bills.
challengeMatrixRows.result: Confirmed - no max term statute exists. The school-specific Title 7 Subtitle 8 contains no contract-term provision; § 5-112 is silent on term; COMAR 13A.06.07 is silent; the MSDE-curated compilation collects no contract-term statute; the DLS 2025 SB 727 'Current Law' description is silent on any contract-term statute. § 7-804's vehicle-life cap is just that — vehicle life, not contract duration (and the Charles County Task Force report describes it as the vehicle standard, not a contracting rule).

challengeMatrixRows.disconfirmingHypothesis: Either (a) a mandatory rebid statute exists for pupil-transportation contracts at term-end (refuting 'conditional'); or (b) renewal/extension is broadly available without bidding (which would make rebid effectively unnecessary even above the threshold).
challengeMatrixRows.challengeSearches: Re-opened § 5-112 in full; tested for 'shall rebid', 'shall recompete', 'shall re-procure', and for broader exceptions (sole source beyond proprietary; broad waiver; board determination). Examined § 5-112(a) exemptions and § 5-112(b) thresholds; checked SFP § 13-109 thresholds and § 13-110 cooperative purchasing; reviewed AACPS solicitation showing renewal options structure.
challengeMatrixRows.result: Confirmed. § 5-112(b) triggers competitive bidding only when a NEW contract above threshold is entered; § 5-112(a) exemptions (books, emergency repairs, cooperative purchasing) limit when bidding is required; nothing in § 5-112 (or elsewhere) auto-mandates rebid on term expiration. 'Conditional' label correctly captures this.

challengeMatrixRows.findingChallenged: Renewal Mechanics - 'No state-prescribed mechanism; controlled by competed contract terms and § 5-112(a) exemptions'
challengeMatrixRows.disconfirmingHypothesis: A state statute or COMAR provision sets an automatic rollover, evergreen renewal, max number of renewals, statutory CPI/fuel escalator, board-approval procedure, or annual-appropriation contingency specific to pupil-transportation contracts.
challengeMatrixRows.challengeSearches: Re-opened § 5-112, COMAR 13A.06.07 (all sections), § 4-205, § 8-410; searched for 'CPI', 'escalator', 'fuel adjustment', 'renewal', 'extension', 'option', 'rollover', 'evergreen', 'mutual agreement', 'board approval' in those sources; checked SFP § 13-110 cooperative purchasing as substitute mechanism.
challengeMatrixRows.result: Confirmed. No state-prescribed renewal mechanic. § 4-205(d)'s county-superintendent written-approval requirement is a procedural validity rule, not a renewal device. The § 5-112(a)(3) cooperative-purchasing pathway exists, but it is an exemption from the bidding requirement, not a renewal mechanism. Practical renewal (e.g., AACPS's 5 single-school-year automatic renewals) comes from competed contract terms, not statute.

challengeMatrixRows.disconfirmingHypothesis: One or more cited statutory URLs (especially the primary § 5-112 URL) does not resolve to the cited section or has been amended/renumbered/superseded since the code-currency date.
challengeMatrixRows.challengeSearches: Re-fetched each official URL on mgaleg.maryland.gov for §§ 4-205, 5-112, 5-205, 7-804, 8-410, SFP §§ 11-202, 13-109; confirmed each StatuteText endpoint returns the cited section text; checked 2025-2026 session-law amendments to those sections via mgaleg.maryland.gov Legislation index and 2026 enacted bills list.
challengeMatrixRows.result: Confirmed verified. Every load-bearing primary statutory URL re-opened on the official Maryland General Assembly site and returned text matching the Stage 1 quotations and characterizations. 2026 SB 292 (Ch. 682, eff. June 1, 2026) was enacted but does not amend any cited contract-term/bidding section and does not affect the analysis. 2026 SB 574 / HB 1493 (Charles County displacement bills) remain pending in committee.

challengeMatrixRows.findingChallenged: Special Education Differentiation - 'No different contract regime'
challengeMatrixRows.disconfirmingHypothesis: Md. Code Ann., Educ. § 8-410 or COMAR 13A.06.07 sets a different term limit, separate bidding regime, separate renewal mechanic, or distinct procurement rule for special-education / IEP / McKinney-Vento / foster-care transportation.
challengeMatrixRows.challengeSearches: Re-opened § 8-410 in full; reviewed COMAR 13A.06.07.17 (Taxicab Drivers Transporting Students with Disabilities) and .18 (SPED Transportation Grant); searched for 'special education contract', 'IEP transportation procurement', 'McKinney-Vento contract Maryland', 'foster care transportation Maryland statute'.
challengeMatrixRows.result: Confirmed. § 8-410 imposes the duty to transport and authorizes MSDE driver/aide standards (including for 'private contract personnel'), with annual reports for systems with 25+ such students - all program/standard-setting, NOT contract cycle. COMAR 13A.06.07.17 (taxicabs) and .18 (grant) impose no separate cycle. Federal IDEA/McKinney-Vento affect duty to transport, not state contract cycle.

challengeMatrixRows.disconfirmingHypothesis: (a) the variance is exaggerated and counties in fact converge on one practice; or (b) one or more cited examples (AACPS, St. Mary's, QACPS, Charles, MCPS) does not actually support the claimed practice.
challengeMatrixRows.challengeSearches: Re-fetched AACPS BidNet solicitation, St. Mary's procedures PDF (pdftotext extraction), Charles County Task Force final report PDF (pdftotext extraction), Charles County CCPS multi-year contract page; cross-checked Queen Anne's 3-year contract via independent web search; re-verified MCPS/Highland State Board decision via WJLA; re-verified Howard County Policy 5200.
challengeMatrixRows.result: Confirmed. Multi-county variance verified across at least four counties: AACPS (7-year + 5x1 auto-renewal tied to vehicle life), St. Mary's (annual renewal for up to 12 years tied to § 7-804), Queen Anne's (3-year, Aug. 26, 2025 - June 30, 2028), Charles (historical 1-year now moving to multi-year). Task Force expressly states 'most jurisdictions offer multi year contracts' and that single-year is uncommon. MCPS/Highland adjudication independently corroborates variance in procurement-manual compliance.

challengeMatrixRows.findingChallenged: Currentness - cited sections not amended/repealed/renumbered since claimed code-currency date
challengeMatrixRows.disconfirmingHypothesis: Recent 2025 or 2026 session laws amended § 5-112, § 7-804, § 8-410, § 4-205(d), § 5-205, or COMAR 13A.06.07 in a way that changes the contract-term/rebid/renewal analysis.
challengeMatrixRows.challengeSearches: Searched 'Maryland 2026 General Assembly student transportation contract bill pupil transportation'; reviewed 2026RS subject index for transportation bills; checked SB 292 / HB 0319 (enacted Ch. 682), SB 574 / HB 1493 (pending), HB 1019 (propane buses, eff. Oct. 1, 2026, contracts beginning FY 2030); cross-checked passedByBoth bills list.
challengeMatrixRows.result: Confirmed currentness. SB 292 (Ch. 682) repeals a sunset on non-school-bus vehicle authority and orders a study - does not affect contract term/rebid/renewal. SB 574 / HB 1493 (Charles County displacement) pending in committee. HB 1019 (propane bus funding) authorizes county boards to enter propane-bus contracts beginning FY 2030 but does not impose term/rebid/renewal limits on those contracts. No 2025 or 2026 amendment to the cited contract-framework sections.

challengeMatrixRows.findingChallenged: Applicability boundary - does the State General Procurement Law (SFP Div. II) reach county boards?
challengeMatrixRows.disconfirmingHypothesis: SFP Div. II's procurement rules (with their own term/renewal limits) actually do reach county boards of education, displacing or layering on top of Educ. § 5-112.
challengeMatrixRows.challengeSearches: Re-opened SFP § 11-202 in full; checked text scope ('expenditure by a unit'); cross-checked § 13-109 (thresholds) and § 13-110 (intergovernmental cooperative purchasing) for any local-board reach.
challengeMatrixRows.result: Confirmed. SFP § 11-202 limits Division II to State 'units' and does not reach county boards of education. § 13-109(a) is referenced only by way of the threshold cross-reference in Educ. § 5-112(b). § 13-110 governs primary-procurement-unit cooperative purchasing - the State analog, not a local-board mandate. Local pupil-transportation procurement is governed by Educ. § 5-112, each board's own procurement manual, and COMAR 13A.06.07.

perFieldVerdicts.note: No statutory maximum found. Re-verified across §§ 5-112, 7-801 through 7-804, 4-205(d), 5-205, 8-410, COMAR 13A.06.07, and via the DLS 2025 SB 727 'Current Law' description. The vehicle-life cap in § 7-804 is the only state-set parameter and is not a contract-term cap.

Conditional. § 5-112(b) requires competitive bidding only for NEW contracts above the $100,000 (or § 13-109(a)) threshold; § 5-112(a)(1)-(3) exemptions (books, emergency repairs, cooperative purchasing) apply; § 5-112(i) void-contract clause re-verified.

No state-prescribed renewal mechanism specific to pupil-transportation contracts. Renewal terms come from competed contract options or § 5-112(a) exemptions (chiefly cooperative purchasing). § 4-205(d) superintendent-approval rule re-verified.

Every primary URL re-opened on the official Maryland General Assembly site (mgaleg.maryland.gov) and returned text matching the Stage 1 quotations. COMAR 13A.06.07.20 vehicle-life rule re-verified on mdrules.elaws.us. 2026 SB 292 (Ch. 682) enacted but does not affect cited sections.

Multi-county variance independently re-verified: AACPS (7+5x1 auto-renewal), St. Mary's (annual renewal for up to 12 years tied to § 7-804), Queen Anne's (3-year), Charles (historical 1-year now moving multi-year), Howard County Policy 5200 framework, MCPS/Highland State Board adjudication. Task Force report directly states most jurisdictions use multi-year. Stage 1 'Howard County Policy 5220 rescinded' aside is downgraded as unverified but is not load-bearing.

§ 8-410 sets program/standard-setting duties (driver/aide standards for 'private contract personnel'; annual reports for systems with 25+ children) but NOT separate contract-cycle rules. COMAR 13A.06.07.17 (taxicab SPED) and .18 (SPED grant) re-verified - same § 5-112 regime applies.

verificationNotes: SOURCE-VALIDATION REVISIT (load-bearing primary citations re-opened on official sources 2026-06-23): (1) Educ. § 5-112 - re-opened on mgaleg.maryland.gov; full text confirms (b)(1) >$100,000 advertising trigger, (a)(1)-(3) exemptions, (g) nonpublic-school participation, (i) void clause; no contract-term cap; no renewal cap. (2) Educ. § 7-804 - re-opened on mgaleg.maryland.gov; full text confirms (b)(1) 12-year default, (b)(2)(i) lists the same 15 counties enumerated in Stage 1 (Allegany, Anne Arundel, Calvert, Caroline, Cecil, Charles, Dorchester, Garrett, Kent, Queen Anne's, St. Mary's, Somerset, Talbot, Wicomico, Worcester), (c) four extension conditions including State Superintendent approval; section addresses vehicle operational lifespan only, NOT contract duration. (3) Educ. § 8-410 - re-opened; (a) duty, (c)(2)(i) MSDE driver/aide standards for 'private contract personnel', (d)(1) 25+ annual reports; no separate contract regime. (4) Educ. § 5-205 - re-opened; bimonthly state-aid certification only; silent on procurement cycles. (5) Educ. § 4-205(d) - re-opened; exact quote 'A contract made by a county board is not valid without the written approval of the county superintendent' confirmed verbatim. (6) SFP § 13-109(a) - re-opened; small-procurement thresholds $100,000 general / $200,000 specified construction confirmed. (7) SFP § 11-202 - re-opened; Division II scope limited to State 'units'; does not reach county boards. (8) COMAR 13A.06.07.20 - re-opened; '12 years' and '15 years or more' caps confirmed verbatim. (9) Charles County Task Force final report - re-verified via pdftotext extraction; 'Charles County operates under single year contracts' and 'the single year model is not common and that most jurisdictions offer multi year contracts' confirmed verbatim; 5% COLA and 8-hour minimum workday agreement confirmed at lines 342-345; Task Force created under Ch. 790, 2023. (10) DLS SB 727 fiscal note - re-verified via pdftotext; 'Current Law' summary confirmed. (11) St. Mary's procedures Paragraph 11 - re-verified verbatim via pdftotext. (12) AACPS BidNet solicitation - re-verified; 7-year initial term, up to 5 additional single-school-year automatic renewals, 90-day non-renewal notice, vehicle-lifecycle limitation confirmed. (13) Queen Anne's 3-year contract - independently re-confirmed via web search; primary citizenportal.ai URL returned 403 to automated fetch but factual claims (3-year contract Aug. 26, 2025 - June 30, 2028; $29.16 hourly; $1.84 mileage; CPI Baltimore-Columbia-Towson) independently corroborated. (14) MCPS/Highland Electric Fleets State Board decision (Nov. 2025) - re-verified via WJLA report; 'arbitrary, unreasonable, and in violation' quote confirmed; multiple independent press sources corroborate. (15) HCPSS Policy 5200 - re-verified; cites Educ. § 5-203, § 7-606, COMAR 13A.06.07. Currentness: 2025-2026 General Assembly check completed; 2026 SB 292 (Ch. 682, eff. June 1, 2026) enacted but does not amend any cited contract-framework section; 2026 SB 574 / HB 1493 (Charles County displacement bills) pending in committee; HB 1019 (propane buses, eff. Oct. 1, 2026, authorizes propane-bus contracts beginning FY 2030) does not impose term/rebid/renewal limits. No amendment to §§ 5-112, 7-804, 8-410, 4-205, 5-205 located. Sources reachable via automated fetch on official endpoints. Two MSDE-domain URLs (the 'MD Annotated Code You Need to Know' PDF and the MSDE 'State Laws and Regulations' SLR.aspx page) returned HTTP 403 to automated fetch on this run (firecrawl token had expired); these are noted as unreachable but are not load-bearing - the negative finding (no MSDE-curated contract-term statute) is independently established by the re-verified primary sources. The citizenportal.ai QACPS link also returned 403 but its factual content was independently corroborated by web search referencing QACPS. The single Stage 1 collateral claim that 'HCPSS Policy 5220 was rescinded' could not be independently re-verified from Policy 5200's own text and is downgraded as unverified - it is not load-bearing for any field finding. As-of date for all current-law claims: 2026-06-23.
sourcesUnreachable: https://marylandpublicschools.org/about/Documents/DBS/Transportation/MDAnnotatedCode_.pdf (HTTP 403 to automated fetch; content corroborated via web-search preview and by independently re-verified primary sources - negative finding stands)

https://citizenportal.ai/articles/5565211/Maryland/Queen-Annes-County-Board-Approves-Three-Year-Contract-for-School-Bus-Services (HTTP 403 to automated fetch; factual content independently corroborated by web-search summary referencing QACPS sources)

adversarialVerdictSummary: confirmed (6/6 fields); minor collateral claim about HCPSS Policy 5220 rescission downgraded as unverified but non-load-bearing; 4 MSDE-domain and aggregator URLs unreachable via automated fetch but content independently corroborated
